passages.explanation: After the Sabbath, as it begins to dawn on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary come to see the tomb. A great earthquake strikes. An angel of the Lord descends from heaven, comes and rolls away the stone, and sits on it. His appearance is like lightning, his clothing white as snow. The guards shake with fear and become like dead men. The angel speaks to the women: don't be afraid. I know you're looking for Jesus who was crucified. He's not here — he is risen, just as he said. Come, see the place where the Lord was lying. Go quickly and tell his disciples: he is risen from the dead, and he goes before you into Galilee. There you will see him. The guards who were posted to prevent a resurrection become the first involuntary witnesses to it. The angel sits on the rolled-away stone like it's a chair — the obstacle has become furniture.

They depart quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and run to bring the disciples word. As they go, Jesus meets them: rejoice! They come, take hold of his feet, and worship him. Jesus tells them: don't be afraid. Go tell my brothers to go into Galilee, and there they will see me. The first people to see the risen Jesus are women — who in that culture couldn't serve as legal witnesses. If the resurrection were fabricated, no one would have invented women as the first witnesses. Jesus calls the disciples 'my brothers' — after they all abandoned him.

While they're going, some of the guards come into the city and report to the chief priests everything that happened. The chief priests assemble with the elders, take counsel, and give a large sum of money to the soldiers. They tell them: say that his disciples came by night and stole him while we slept. If this comes to the governor's ears, we'll persuade him and keep you out of trouble. The soldiers take the money and do as they're told. This story is widely spread among the Jews to this day. The cover story is self-defeating: if the guards were sleeping, how would they know it was the disciples who took the body? The authorities don't deny the empty tomb — they just offer an alternative explanation for it.

The eleven disciples go to Galilee, to the mountain where Jesus had directed them. When they see him, they worship him — but some doubt. Jesus comes and speaks to them: all authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth. Go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all things that I commanded you. Behold, I am with you always, even to the end of the age. Matthew's gospel ends where it began — with Immanuel, God with us. The commission is global: all nations, not just Israel. The authority is total: heaven and earth. The promise is permanent: to the end of the age. And even in this climactic moment, some doubt. The mission doesn't wait for perfect faith — it begins with the faith that shows up.
